hints: Let m(x)=E[N_x]; condition on X₁ to get m(x)=1+∫₀ˣ m(x−t)dt.
solution: Let $m(x)=\mathbb{E}[N_x]$. Conditioning on $X_1$, $m(x)=1+\int_0^x m(x-t)\,dt$, so $m'(x)=m(x)$ with $m(0)=1$, giving $m(x)=e^x$. Thus $\mathbb{E}[N]=e^{\ln2}=2.$

hints: X−Y−Z is normal with mean 12−12−12=−12 and variance 12+12+12=36.
The chest value is Binomial(20,1/2) with mean 10; bidding above 10 is dominated.
Count pure equilibria by how many players bid at the top and the tie-break incentives.
Sum the cases over k top-bidders.
Going from i to i+1 distinct toys is geometric with success (5−i)/5.
solution: Collecting the $(i{+}1)$-th new toy takes a geometric mean of $\tfrac{5}{5-i}$ boxes, so $\mathbb{E}=5\sum_{j=1}^{5}\tfrac1j=5\cdot\tfrac{137}{60}=\tfrac{137}{12}.$

hints: The left side has only prime factors 2 and 3; the right only 5.
solution: Price is unchanged iff $(1.2)^k(0.8)^{10-k}=1$, i.e. $6^k4^{10-k}=5^{10}$. The left has only factors $2,3$ and the right only $5$, so no $k$ satisfies it: the probability is $0.$

hints: Implied USD→GBP via EUR is 1.5/0.85≈1.76, but the direct rate is 1.8 — trade USD→GBP first.
solution: The direct $1\,\text{USD}=1.8\,\text{GBP}$ beats the cross rate via EUR. Cycling $1000\,\text{USD}\to1800\,\text{GBP}\to\tfrac{1800}{1.5}=1200\,\text{EUR}\to1200(0.85)=1020\,\text{USD}$ yields a profit of $\$20.$

hints: Friend (first) wins with prob p/(1−(1−p)(1−q)); set equal to 1/2.
solution: The first player wins with probability $\dfrac{p}{1-(1-p)(1-q)}$. Setting this to $\tfrac12$ with $p=0.4$ gives $1-0.6(1-q)=0.8$, so $0.6(1-q)=0.2$ and $q=\tfrac23.$

hints: Unread at box 1 happens if mail is in box 1 but unnoticed (1/3·2/3) or in another box (2/3).
solution: The observation (checked box 1, nothing read) occurs with probability $\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23=\tfrac29$ if the mail is in box 1 (unnoticed) and $\tfrac23$ otherwise. By Bayes, $P(\text{box 1})=\dfrac{2/9}{2/9+6/9}=\dfrac14.$

hints: Binary search halves the range each guess.
solution: Binary search halves the candidate range each guess, needing $2^n\ge1000$. Since $2^{10}=1024\ge1000>512$, the answer is $n=10.$

hints: Dropping S from 42 toward K=36 increases the call's vega magnitude.
But you're short the call, so your position's vega decreases (more negative): −1.
solution: Vega is maximized near the money, so as $S$ falls from $42$ toward $K=36$ the call's vega grows. Since you sold the call, your position vega is negative and becomes more negative — it decreases, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: For choosing m of n labeled 1..n, E[max]=m(n+1)/(m+1).
solution: The expected maximum when choosing $m$ of $\{1,\dots,n\}$ is $\dfrac{m(n+1)}{m+1}$. With $m=10,n=20$: $\dfrac{10\cdot21}{11}=\dfrac{210}{11}.$

hints: Ask 'is the sequence in set A?'; then guess one element of the indicated side.
Either answer leaves a uniform set, so P(correct)=1/2¹⁰⁰+1/2¹⁰⁰.
solution: With one yes/no question you split the $2^{100}$ sequences into a set and its complement, then guess one element on the indicated side. Each correct guess contributes $\tfrac1{2^{100}}$, totaling $2\cdot2^{-100}=2^{-99}$. So $a=2,b=99$ and $ab=198.$
